chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Basics of Wireless Systems Main Parts (SISO): Tx, Wireless Channel & Rx
Main Parts (SISO): Tx, Wireless Channel & Rx
A wireless system can be partitioned into 3 main parts:
1 Tx (a " source " that sends/transmits some information using wave
propagation)
2 Wireless Channel (the physical propagation paths )
3 Rx ( a " sink " that receives the transmitted waves)
and the objective in general is
I to increase the communication speed (which is known as channel
capacity)
without sacricing the quality of service (for a given energy +
bandwidth)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 3 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Basics of Wireless Systems Classications of Wireless Systems
Classications of Wireless Systems
There are many classications of wireless systems. For instance:
1 according to the bandwidth/carrier: narrowband or wideband
2 according to the spreading capabilities : conventional or spread
spectrum
3 according to the number of carriers : single carrier or multicarrier
4 according to the "generation": 1G, 2G, 3G , 3G+
5 according to the "access": TDMA, FDMA, CDMA.
The overall aims:
I speed = ",
I but maintaining reliability (quality of service) & spectral e¢ ciency
(EUE,BUE)
Remember:
I EUE= Energy Utilisation E¢ ciency , Energy per bit (Eb )
N0
I BUE= Bandwidth Utilsation E¢ ciency , Bandwidth (B )
bit rate (rb )
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 4 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Basics of Wireless Systems Classications of Wireless Systems
Wireless Systems have evolved over the years to the latest
developments in Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO)
wireless systems and technologies.
N.B.:
The current speed is expected to increase by the utilisation of the new
technology of MIMO wireless systems and this gives rise to a new
classication which super-sets all the above.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 5 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
SIMO, MISO and MIMO: Two Modelling Philosophies
SIMO, MISO, MIMO: Two Modelling Philosophies
1 Non-Parametric :
The antennas are assumed to work independently and thus the
various links/channels from antenna-to-antenna are represented by
single complex numbers.
2 Parametric:
All antennas are "working together" as a single entity (an antenna
array system) and the various links/channels are mathematically
modelled as a vector-function of the array geometry and the
electromagnetic wave propagation.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 8 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
SIMO, MISO and MIMO: Two Modelling Philosophies
(cont.)
Although a non-parametric approach is suitable for single antenna
systems (i.e. SISO), it does not properly t multiple antennas since it
I ignores the Cartesian coordinates and orientations of Tx and Rx (i.e.
ignoring the geometry/location of the multiple antennas),
I ignores the directions of the signals,
I ignores propagation models (planewaves or spherical waves),
I etc.
Remember: SISO - one complex number β per path

EM-wave Refresher Revisiting the Electric and Magnetic Fields
We can remove theRe operator by using a single-carrier oscillator in the Tx/Rx of
the system with a phase shifter of 90, producing so two parallel carrier signals: the
"I" and "Q" signals.
I The I (inphase) signal is the cosine of the carrier frequencyFc, i.e. cos(2πFct), and
I the Q (Quadrature) signal is the sine of the carrier frequency, that is 90 wrt cosine,
i.e. sin(2πFct),
The two parallel carrier signals are common in all modern wireless systems.
Although these two (I and Q) signals are "real", we represent them as a single
"complex signal".
In this case, at a pointr = [x, y, z]T (withR =krk) in space and at timet, the
Electric and Magnetic FieldsE(r, t) andH(r, t), respectively, can be represented

Denition (Array System)
A wireless system is known as an array system if it employs an array
of N > 1 sensors (transducing elements, receivers, antennas, etc)
distributed in our 3-dimensional real space with a common reference
point (common origin).

The Structure of the Array Response Vector
From now on in this presentation the vectorS will represent all multiple
antenna wireless systems, i.e.

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Antenna Array Comms The Structure of the Array Manifold Vector
(cont.)
Array Manifold: the locus of all manifold vectors for every value of
the parameter of interest ( e.g. direction) is a continuum (i.e. a
geometrical object such as a curve, a surface, etc) lying in an
N-dimensional complex space. This "object" ("continuum") is
dened as the "Array Manifold".
In an array system the manifold (array manifold) can be calculated
(and stored) from only the knowledge of the locations and directional
characteristics of the sensors.
Various forms of S have di¤erent dimensions but always a profound
mathematical structure.
This leads to Di¤erential Geometry which complements the statistical
signal processing and Shannon s communication theory.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 51 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Antenna Array Comms Di¤erential Geometry and Fundamental Questions
Di¤erential Geometry
Di¤erential geometry is a branch of mathematics that is concerned
with the application of di¤erential calculus for the investigation of the
properties of geometric curves , surfaces and other objects known
as  manifolds .
Manifolds have a deep and profound mathematical structure and
have been an area of intense pure mathematical analysis .
p7!mathematical object
In Physics, Albert Einstein (Nobel 1921) used di¤erential geometry to
express his general theory of relativity
I where the universe is a smooth manifold equipped with
pseudo-Riemannian metric (described the curvature of space-time).
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 52 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Antenna Array Comms Di¤erential Geometry and Fundamental Questions
Fundamental Questions
Di¤. Geom. helps answering some
fundamental questions such as:
Q1 Is it possible to express a wireless
system as a space curve or a surface
(or a manifold - in general)?
Q2 Is it possible to analyse a wireless
system by analysing a curve or a
surface?
Q3 Is it possible to design a wireless
system by designing a curve or a
surface?
Q4 What do we stand to gain by
expressing wireless systems as
mathematical objects such as curves
or surfaces?
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 53 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
Antenna Array Comms Di¤erential Geometry and Fundamental Questions
Conclusion
The overall conclusions that can be drawn are:
1) Di¤erential Geometry goes hand-in-hand with SIMO , MISO and
MIMO
2) Di¤erential Geometry is at odds with SISO.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 54 / 72

chunks.title: Introductory Overview
chunks.content:
5G Revolutionary Changes
5G Revolutionary Changes:
In addition to delivering faster connections and greater capacity, some
very important revolutionary advantages of 5G are:
I Latency ( response time over the wireless network is reduced from
30ms to 1ms)
I New Communications families (emBC, mMC, mCC)
I New unied air-interface: New Radio (NR)
I massive MIMO
I mmWave Beamforming and beamtracking
I advanced channel coding (multi-edge LDPC and CRC-aided polar)
MIMO, beamforming, beamtracking and massive-MIMO :
improve spectral e¢ ciency, capacity
beamforming is equivalent to power amplication in space: it involves
receiving a desired signal in space while suppressing other signals (also
in space) as unwanted interference.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: An Introductory Overview v.24a 57 / 72

chunks.title: Diversity Theory
chunks.content:
Diversity: Denition
Diversity: Denition
In general, the term "diversity system" refers to a system in which
there are available two or more closely similar copies of some desired
signal.
The  Diversity Combining(or brie y "diversity") Concept:
Diversity is dened as a general technique that utilizes two or more
copies of a signal with varying degrees of noise/interference e¤ects
to achieve, by selection or a combination scheme, higher degree of
message-recovery performance than that achievable by any one of the
individual copies separately.
The concept of diversity was introduced in 1959 by Brennan
[D. G. Brennan, "Linear Diversity Combining Techniques",
Proceedings of the IRE , vol.47, no.6, pp.1075-1102, 1959]
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: Diversity Theory v.24 3 / 46

Classication of Diversity Techniques/Systems
Classication of Diversity Techniques/Systems
In diversity systems, there are several known methods of obtaining
two or more signals si , in order to be combined using one of the
diversity combining rules, to obtain an improved signal.
According to the method of obtaining copies of the same signal the
diversity systems and their associated techniques are classied as
follows:
I Multi-Path Diversity
I Time Diversity
I Frequency Diversity
I Polarization Diversity
I Code Diversity
I Space Diversity

The delay spread is a measure of the multipath richness of a
wireless channel.
I In general, it can be interpreted as the di¤erence between the time of
arrival of the earliest signicant multipath component and the time of
arrival of the latest signicant multipath components.
In modern wireless systems the aim is to resolve multipaths, to
estimate them and nally to utilise them.

Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: Diversity Theory v.24 30 / 46

chunks.title: Diversity Theory
chunks.content:
Multipath Diversity RAKE Rx: An Optimum Multipath Diversity Rx
In this case the diversity branches are replaced by correlation receivers
(or matched lters). This is known as multipath diversity, the overall
structure as RAKE receiver and the associated diversity branches (i.e.
correlators) as RAKE ngers .
Thus, in this way, a plurality of parallel correlation receivers (ngers)
is used with each of them synchronised to a di¤erent path. Then the
outputs of these ngers are combined according to a diversity
combining criterion/rule (followed by a decision device).
RAKE receiver is an optimum coherent receiver for multipath
channels and it was introduced in 1958 by Price and Green
[R. Price and P. E. Green, "A Communication Technique for
Multipath Channels", Proceedings of IRE, pp.555-570, March 1958]
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: Diversity Theory v.24 31 / 46

chunks.title: Diversity Theory
chunks.content:
Multipath Diversity RAKE Rx: An Optimum Multipath Diversity Rx
The generic structure of a RAKE receiver is below.
Hence, if one path (or more) is lost (because its associated path
gain coe¢ cient becomes very small) the output SNR remains in an
optimum level.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: Diversity Theory v.24 32 / 46

chunks.title: Diversity Theory
chunks.content:
Multipath Diversity RAKE Rx: An Optimum Multipath Diversity Rx
A nger is, by itself, an optimum coherent receiver (correlator or
matched lter receiver) while, according to Brennan s original paper
[Brennan 1959] there are four main diversity combining rules to
combine the outputs of the ngers.
Thus, there are four classes of RAKE receivers - based on diversity
combining rules:
1 RAKE with maximum-ratio combining (this is the best of these four
types and provides the highest output SNR - but is more complex);
2 RAKE with selection combining (RAKE-SC);
3 RAKE with equal gain combining (RAKE-EGC);
4 RAKE with scanning combining (RAKE SCC)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: Diversity Theory v.24 33 / 46

it has a number of  ngers (One Rake- ngerper path)

chunks.title: Diversity Theory
chunks.content:
Multipath Diversity Examples Example 2: SSS (Rake and Correlation Rxs)
Example 2: SSS (Rake and Correlation Receivers)
In a wireless channel, in addition to the noise, the transmitted signal
is also a¤ected by fading e¤ects (due to multipath propagation and
Doppler e¤ects).
If the paths in a multipath channel can be separated (i.e.
Tspread > Tc ) then there are two main ways to exploit the multipath
propagation channel for enhancing the performance of the receiver,
using the auto-correlation properties of the PN-codes:
1 to use a correlation receiver (CORRELATION Rx) that is perfectly
synchronised to one of the paths, i.e. correct delay (no diversity).
2 to employ the diversity concept (RAKE Rx).
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: Diversity Theory v.24 36 / 46

chunks.title: Diversity Theory
chunks.content:
Multipath Diversity Examples Example 2: SSS (Rake and Correlation Rxs)
Example 2 (cont): Correlation Rx (no diversity)
A correlation receiver (no diversity) is perfectly synchronised to
one of the paths (i.e. correct delay)
I This will suppress all the other received paths based on the
correlation properties of the PN-code
(with m-sequences having the best auto-correlation properties).
The gure below shows a representative example of the correlation
function at the input and at the output of a correlator operating in
the presence of 3 paths.

chunks.title: SIMO, MISO, MIMO
chunks.content:
Space Selectivity in Wireless Channels Space-Selective Fading
Space-Selective Fading
A wireless receiver is located (and moves) in our 3D real space.
In addition to delay-spread (causing frequency-selective fading) and
Doppler-spread (causing time-selective fading) there is also
angle-spread
Angle Spread causes Space-Selective fading
Note that a channel has
I space-selectivity if it varies (i.e. its transfer function varies) as a
function of space and
I spatial-coherence if its transfer function does not vary as a function
of space over a specied distance ( Dcoh ) of interest
where
Dcoh = is known as coherence distance . (10)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: SIMO, MISO and MIMO v.19c3 11 / 87

chunks.title: SIMO, MISO, MIMO
chunks.content:
Space Selectivity in Wireless Channels Space-Selective Fading
jH(r )j V0 forjr r0j Dcoh
2 (11)
I In other words a wireless channel has spatial coherence if the envelope
of the carrier remains constant over a spatial displacement of the
receiver.
I Dcoh represents the largest distance that a wireless receiver can move
with the channel appearing to be static/constant.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: SIMO, MISO and MIMO v.19c3 12 / 87

chunks.title: SIMO, MISO, MIMO
chunks.content:
Space Selectivity in Wireless Channels Small-Scale and Large-Scale fading
Small-Scale and Large-Scale fading
If the displacement of the receiver is greater than Dcoh then the
channel experiences small-scale fading (this is due to multipaths
added constructively or destructively).
If this displacement is very large (i.e. corresponds to a very large
number of wavelengths) then the channel experiences large-scale
fading (this is due to path loss over large distances and shadowing
by large objects - it is typically independent of frequency)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: SIMO, MISO and MIMO v.19c3 13 / 87

Wireless SIMO Channels Multi-path SIMO Channel Modelling
Multi-path SIMO Channel Modelling
Let us assume that the transmitted signal arrives at the reference
point of an array receiver via L paths (multipaths).
Consider that the `th path arrives at the array from direction ( θ`, φ`)
with channel propagation parameters β` and τ` representing the
complex path gain and path-delay, respectively.

chunks.title: SIMO, MISO, MIMO
chunks.content:
Wireless MISO Channels Transmit Diversity
Transmit Diversity
Provides diversity benets to a mobile using base station antenna
array for frequency division duplexing (FDD) schemes. Cost is shared
among di¤erent users.
Order of diversity can be increased when used with other conventional
forms of diversity (e.g. multipath diversity).
Two main types of diversity combining techniques in 3G:
I Transmit diversity with feedback from receiver (close loop)
I Transmit diversity without feedback from receiver (open loop)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: SIMO, MISO and MIMO v.19c3 54 / 87

chunks.title: SIMO, MISO, MIMO
chunks.content:
Wireless MISO Channels Transmit Diversity
Transmit Diversity: "Close Loop"
The transmitter transmits some pilot signals
The mobile (based on this pilot signals) estimates the Channel State
Information (CSI), i.e. channel parameters.
The mobile transmits the CSI to the BS (uplink)
The base station generates the weights and transmits data to the
mobile.
I That is a beamformer is formed which steers its main lobe towards the
mobile
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE.401: SIMO, MISO and MIMO v.19c3 55 / 87

By observing a vector-signal x(t) = Sm(t)+n(t) using an array
system the aim is to obtain information about a signal environment.
There are three general problems to solve.

The Detection Problem: Basic Detection Theory
The  DetectionProblem: Basic Detection Theory
Problem
To determine the parameter M
i.e. to determine the number of signals and thus the dimensions of
the vectors/matrices m (t), S, Rmm and M
In other words, to detect how many emitting sources/transmitters are
present in an array environment
i.e. to detect the presence of M sources
Solution
To use optimum Detection and Decision Theory
Note-1: The main optimum decision criteria are summarised in
Appendix-A (at the end of this handout).
Note-2: The ML decision criterion will be employed in this section.

If the distributions of the observed signal r (t) for various hypotheses are
known
then the problem of choosing one of many (say M) hypotheses is
translated to make a decision about one of the M distributions after
having observed r (t).

chunks.content:
The Detection Problem: Basic Detection Theory Information Theoretic Criteria
Information Theoretic Criteria
The information theoretic criteria for model selection, introduced by
I Akaike ( Information Theory Symposium 1973, Automatic Control 1974 )
I Schwartz ( Estimating the dimension of a model, Ann. Stat, 1978 ), and
I Rissanen ( Modeling by shortest datad escription, Automatica, 1978 )
address the following general problem.
Problem
Given a set of L observations (L snapshots), i.e. data,
X = [ x (t1), x(t2)...., x(tL)g
and a family of models , that is, a parameterised family of probability
densities pdf( XjHi ),8i , select the model that best ts the data (i.e.
the model that ts the set of L observations) .
Remember that the conditional probability densities pdf( XjHi ) ,8i,
are known as Likelihood Functions (LF)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: Array Rx s for SIMO & MIMO v.19c4 31 / 132

chunks.title: Array Receivers, SIMO, MIMO
chunks.ref: ACT_4 Array Receivers, SIMO, MIMO, page 33
chunks.content:
The Detection Problem: Basic Detection Theory Information Theoretic Criteria
MDL Criterion
Inspired by Akaike s pioneering work, Schwartz and Rissanen
approached the problem from quite di¤erent points of view.
I Schwartz s approach is based on Bayesian arguments (Bayes criterion).
He assumed that each competing model can be assigned a prior
probability, and proposed to select the model that yields the maximum
posterior probability.
I Rissanen s approach is based on information theoretic arguments. Since
each model can be used to encode the observed data, Rissanen
proposed to select the model that yields the minimum code length.
It turns out that in the large-sample limit, both Schwartz s and
Rissanen s approaches yield the same criterion, (known as MDL 2
criterion)
2MDL: Minimum Description Length
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: Array Rx s for SIMO & MIMO v.19c4 33 / 132

The Detection Problem: Basic Detection Theory Information Theoretic Criteria
Example
Consider a ULA of 7 antennas with halfwavelength spacing (i.e. N = 7)
operating in the presence of two sources with directions-of-arrivals 20
and 25. The signal-to-noise ratio is 10 dB. Using L = 100 snapshots (i.e.
samples), the resulted eigenvalues of the sample-covariance matrix are:
21.2359, 2.1717, 1.4279, 1.0979, 1,0544, 0.9432, and 0.7324.
Observing the gradual decrease of the eigenvalues it is clear that the
separation of the smallest eigenvalues from the large ones is a
di¢ cult task.

The Estimation Problem: Basic Estimation Theory The Subspace-type Approach
The Signal-Subspace Approaches
In this type of algorithms the parameter M is assumed known
(M < N) and involves in some way, or another, two concepts:
i) the concept of the " signal-subspace" associated with the observed
signal-vector x (t) and its properties
F This is an unknown linear subspace of dimensionality equal M -
embedded in an N-dimensional ( M < N)
ii) the concept of the " manifold" associated with the system/problem s
characteristicsin the case of arrays known as "array manifold". It is
independent of the noisy observed signal-vector x(t) and its properties.
F This is a non-linear subspace (e.g. a curve, surface etc) - embedded in
an N-dimensional observation space

chunks.content:
The Estimation Problem: Basic Estimation Theory The Concept of the Manifold
By recording t he locus of the manifold vectors as a function of the
parameter p ( e.g. direction), a continuum (i.e. a geometrical
object such as a curve) is formed lying in an N-dimensional space .
This geometrical object (locus of manifold vectors i.e. S (p),8p) is
known as the array manifold .
In an array system the manifold (array manifold) can be calculated
(and stored) from only the knowledge of the locations and directional
characteristics of the sensors.
Let S (p)2 C N be the manifold vector of a system of N dimensions
(e.g. of an array of N sensors) where p is a generic system parameter.
This is a single-parameter vector function and as p varies the point
S (p) will trace out a curve A (see gure), embedded in an
N-dimensional space C N .

chunks.ref: ACT_4 Array Receivers, SIMO, MIMO, page 85
chunks.content:
The Estimation Problem: Basic Estimation Theory Spatial Smoothing (Preprocessing)
Spatial Smoothing (Preprocessing)
If the some message signals m1(t), m1(t), ..., mM (t) are "coherent"
(i.e. fully correlated, which means that the correlation coe¢ cient is
equal to 1) then the MUSIC algorithm fails to estimate the coherent
signal directions. In this case the rank of Rsignal is not full rank and
the signal subspace dimension is less than M.
If the array is uniform linear array of halfwavelength spacing then this
can be "corrected" using the spatial smoothing algorithm before using
MUsIC. This implies that
1 We partition the array to sub-arrays.
2 For each subarray we form the covariance matrix.
3 We take the average of all these subarray covariance matrices to form
the Rxx ,smooth
4 We apply MUSIC using the Rxx ,smooth and anyone of the subarrays

switched beamformer : there is a nite number of xed array
patterns and the system chooses one of them to maximise signal
strength (the one with main lobe closer to the desired user/signal)
and switches from one to beam to another as the user/signal moves
throughout the sector).
adaptive beamformer (or adaptive array): array patterns are
adjusted automatically (main lobe extending towards a user/signal
with a null directed towards a cochannel user/signal)
switched beamformer adaptive beamformer Adaptive for 2 cochannel signals/users

A beamformer is an array system which receives a  desiredsignal
and suppresses (according to a criterion) co-channel interference
and noise e¤ects, by synthesizing an array pattern with high-gain
towards the DOA of the desired signal and deep nulls towards the
DOAs of the interfering signals (adaptive arrays).

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
Increasing
the Degrees of Freedom
I
ncreasing the Degrees-of-Freedom
There
is an increased interest for beamformer-based communications
in 5G. Issues:
I high path loss,
I very dense co-channel interference environment.
N.B.:This means that the number of sources/signals M > N
I multipath e¤ects in frequency selective channels.
Solutions :
1 to
employ massive MIMO ,
F That is, if M > N then we can increase N so that M < N,
i.e. we add more hardware (HW) but also we increase the
computational complexity .
2 to
employ statiotemporal algorithms (manifold extenders)
F That is, if M > N then we can keep N x but we can use sophisticated
algorithms to increase the observation space from N to N.Next - so
that M < N.Next ,
i.e. only computational complexity is increased
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 3 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
Increasing
the Degrees of Freedom
S
olution-1
to
employ massive MIMO , i.e. to increase the "degrees-of-freedom"
by increasing N (remember: M < N)
I Multiple-antenna (MIMO): the technology is becoming mature for
wireless communications
I It has been incorporated into wireless broadband standards like LTE
and Wi-Fi.
I There are two cases:
F Non-parametric massive: The antennas are not working together but
are independent units. This is a problematic approach
(N=") number-of-unknowns=")
F Parametric massive: based on array processing (antennas are working
together as a single unit. This is a good approach
(N=") number-of-unknowns6=").
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 4 / 124

chunks.content:
Increasing
the Degrees of Freedom
S
olution-2
to
use "extended array manifolds"
(otherwise known as " S patiot emporal-ar ray ( STAR ) manifolds ")
Spacial cases of extended manifolds:
I virtual array manifolds (based on virtual arrays), or
I virtual STAR algorithms .
In this case we keep the number of antennas N xed but we extend
the observation space from N to N.Next
I
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 5 / 124

duction
Massive MIMO: Introduction
Massive MIMO also known as Large-Scale Antenna Systems, Very Large
MIMO, Hyper MIMO and Full-Dimension MIMO
D
enition (MIMO and maMI)
MIMO
(multiple input, multiple output) is an antenna array
technology for wireless systems (e.g. wireless comms or radar) where
antenna arrays are used at both the transmitter (source) and receiver
(destination).
However, there is no a clear denition when a MIMO system is
dened as "massive MIMO" (maMI)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 9 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
Massive
MIMO Massive
MIMO (maMI): What and Why?
Massive MIMO: What and Why?
maMI :
makes a clean break with current practice through the use of
a very large number of antennas N (e.g., hundreds or thousands)
that are operated fully coherently and adaptively.
Extra antennas
+
focusing the transmission and reception of signal energy into
ever-smaller regions of space
+
huge improvements in throughput and energy e¢ ciency
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 12 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
Massive
MIMO Massive
MIMO (maMI): What and Why?
MaMI
was originally
envisioned for time
division duplex (TDD)
operation (channel
estimation is carried out
only on the uplink and the
estimated parameters are
used in the downlink),
MaMI
can potentially be
applied also in frequency
division duplex (FDD)
operation (channel
estimation can be carried
out on both uplink and
downlink).
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 13 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
Massive
MIMO Massive
MIMO (maMI): What and Why?
The
more antennas N the transmitter/receiver is equipped with,
I the more the possible signal paths/users/sources/Tx ( M) can be
F detected,
F resolved and
F their parameters can be estimated
and
I the better the performance in terms of data rate and link reliability.
The p
rice to pay is
I increased complexity of the hardware (number of antennas, number of
RF-chains, etc) and
I the complexity (longer vectors and bigger matrices to handle) and
energy consumption of the signal processing at both ends.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 14 / 124

ry of Main Advantages and Challenges

ain Advantages/Benets of MaMIMO
Increased data
rate .
Signi
cant reduction in air-latency .
Simpli
es the MAC (media access control) layer.
Imp
roved energy e¢ ciency .
Imp
roved interference suppression
(robustness to interference and intentional jamming)

massive MIMO renders many traditional research problems
irrelevant, it uncovers entirely new problems that urgently need

ain Challenges of maMIMO

rdware complexity =" ) Low cost RF chains required.

duction
Spatiotemporal Communications
Spatiotemp
oral (Space-Time) Communications can be employed in
TDMA/FDMA, CDMA, OFDMA and NR type systems
N.B.:
I In TDMA/FDMA the
signal is not spread and only few (mainly
one
or two - i.e. M < N) strong cochannel interferences (CCI) are present
when the system employs channel reuse between cells.
The array can be used to null (remove/reduce)
these few
interferences
I In CDMA all active users use the same bandwidth and are separated
by employing di¤erent PN-codes to reduce/remove the MAI
interference from other user.
i.e. in a CDMA environment the array has to deal with a very
large number of weak interferences (M > N).

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures

mmW
ave Beamformers Analogue
mmWave Beamformer
Analogue mmWave Beamformers
In
analogue beamforming, one baseband port feeds an analogue
beamforming network where the beamforming weights are applied
either directly on the analog baseband components, at some
intermediate frequency, or at RF. For example, an RF beamforming
network may consist of several phase shifters, one per antenna
element, and optionally also variable gain ampliers.
In
any case, an analogue beamforming network typically generates
physical beams but cannot generate a complex beam pattern.
Especially in a multi-user environment this can lead to interference, if
pure beam separation is not su¢ cient.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 79 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
mmW
ave Beamformers Comments
C
omments
A
beamforming receiver provides spatial selectivity (i.e. high gain
reception of signals in desired directions while suppressing
signals in other directions ).
Each
individual antenna element, however, does not provide much
spatial selectivity. For a digital beamforming receiver this means that
each signal path, extending from respective antenna element all the
way to the baseband port, will have to accommodate desired as well
as undesired signals.
Thus,
to handle strong undesired signals, requirements on dynamic
range will be high for all blocks in the signal path and that will
have a corresponding impact on power consumption . In an analog
beamformer, however, the beamforming may be carried out already at
RF and thus all subsequent blocks will need less dynamic range
compared to the digital beamforming receiver.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 85 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
App
endix: 5G Intro
duction
Introduction
D
enition
5G
is the 5th generation of mobile networks.
This
is a signicant evolution of todays 4G LTE networks. There are
also those who believe that this not simply an "evolution" but a
"revolution" (4th industrial revolution, 5G ecosystem).
5G
has been designed to meet the very large growth in data and
connectivity of today s modern society, the internet of things with
billions of connected devices, and tomorrow s innovations.
5G
will initially operate in conjunction with existing 4G networks
before evolving to fully standalone networks in subsequent releases
and coverage expansions.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 100 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
App
endix: 5G Intro
duction
D
enitions
1 F
emtocell: A femtocell is a small, low-power cellular base station,
typically designed for use in a home or small business. A broader term
which is more widespread in the industry is small cell, with femtocell
as a subset. It is also called femto AccessPoint (AP).
2 F
antom Cell: (new concept in LTE-B)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) EE401: ST, maMI and mmWave v.21a 102 / 124

chunks.title: Extended Array Architectures
chunks.content:
App
endix: 5G Revolutiona
ry Changes
Revolutionary Changes: Latency
In
addition to delivering faster connections and greater capacity, a
very important advantage of 5G is the fast response time referred to
as latency.
Latency
is the time taken for devices to respond to each other over
the wireless network.
E
xamples (Latency)
3G
networks had a typical response time of 100 milliseconds,
4G
is around 30 milliseconds and
5G
will be as low as 1 millisecond
(this is virtually instantaneous - opening up a new world of connected
applications)

endix: 5G Revolutiona
ry Changes
Revolutionary Changes: New Comm. Families
Enhanced
Mobile Broadband communications (emBC)  i.e.
mobile communication links of very high bandwidth (broadband) thus
delivering very high speeds per user (multi Gbits/sec). For instance,
mobile users with UHD screens need this family of comm. links.
Massive
Machine Communications (mMC) , - i.e. simultaneous
comm links to potentially millions of machines (e.g. smart cities).
Mission
Critical Communications (mCC)  i.e. communication
links of high reliability and low latency (e.g. comm links for
self-driving cars or aerospace industry)

uses MIMO antenna schemes (maximizing the reuse of scarce

endix: 5G New

named 5G as "New Radio " (NR)
NR
supports OFDM-family with
I subcarrier spacing : 152m kHz (Note: In LTE-4G this is 15kHz)
F m = 0 or positive integers: 15 , 30, 60, 120, 240, 480 kHz

I NR supports spectrum below and above 6GHz (this could be licensed
or unlicenced)

This course is concerned with the problem of Locating and Tracking

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
More on Localisation Applications
More on Localisation Applications
Some of the most interesting positioning application areas have
emerged in Wireless Communications . The most prominent:
I FCC (Federal Communications Commission) which requires that the
precise location of all enhanced 911 (E911) emergency calls be
automatically determined.
F FCC Mandate: all handsets sold be location compatible.
I European Recommendation E112
I Both E911 and E112 require that wireless providers should be able to
locate within tens of meters users of emergency calls .
Other applications (besides E911 and E112 services)
I Vehicle navigation
I Network optimisation
I Resource allocation
I Automatic billing
I Ubiquitous computing (e.g. for accessing personal info, corporate data,
share resources, anywhere)
I location-aware computing
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 8 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation

Localisation Algorithms TOA Localisation
Note: TOA Requirements
I TOA approach requires accurate synchronisation between the Rx1,
Rx2, Rx3 and Tx clocks .
F This requirement ensures that the estimated ρ1, ρ2, ρ3 are good
approximations of the actual distances.
I Many of the current wireless system standards only require tight
timing synchronisation amongst BSs .
F The MS clock itself might have a drift that can be even a few
microseconds.
F This drift directly produces errors in ρ1, ρ2, ρ3 and, consequently,
errors in the location estimate of the TOA method.

I TDOA approach requires accurate synchronisation only amongst
the BSs clocks . i.e it requires a synchronous network
F This requirement ensures that the estimated r12 , r23 , r13 are good
approximations of the actual distances.
I A cellular network is not a synchronous network.
F Therefore, TDOA can not be used directly (it su¤ers from
synchronisation errors amongst BSs)
F GSM employs a quite expensive solution known as E-OTD (Enhanced
Observed Time Di¤erence)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 31 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Localisation Algorithms DOA Localisation
DOA Localisation
DOA Localisation:
I Each Rx uses an antenna array
I Each Rx employs a DOA algorithm (e.g. MUSIC) to estimate the Tx s
DOA. By combining the DOA estimates from two di¤erent Rx s (e.g.
θ1, θ2 say) an estimate one range equation (e.g ρ1) of Tx location can
be found and consequently an estimate of the Tx-location can be
obtained.
DOA localisation
I is a better algorithm than TOA, TDOA and RSSI,
I does not require clock synchronisation between the Rx s or between the
Rx s and Tx.
Next gure shows a representative example of
I 3 Rx s (or 3 BS s) at known locations (see three yellow circles) where
each Rx uses an antenna array and a DOA algorithm.
I 1 Tx (or 1 MS) at unknown location (see one red circle), which should
be estimated.

chunks.content:
Localisation Algorithms Hybrid Localisation
Hybrid Localisation
In TOA, TDOA and DOA approaches two or more BS are employed
in the MS location estimation.
In some situations, for example.
I MS much closer to one BS,
I BS antenna array is surrounded by many scatterers
an alternative (hybrid) procedure may be used
Hybrid Localisation combines, for instance, TOA and DOA estimates
using the following 3-step procedure.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 61 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation

Localisation Algorithms Fingerprinting Localisation
One way of solving the multipath problem is to consider multipath
signal characteristics as "ngerprintings" of MS
Comparing received signal patterns/characteristics by a BS with
signal characteristics stored in a database ( using pattern matching
algorithms known as ngerprintings )
I e.g. signal levels, time delays, multipath delay prole
Needs one BS/Rx with several multipath copies.

chunks.content:

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Localisation Sources of Errors
Localisation Sources of Errors
Multipaths
I RSS: 30-40dB variation over distances in the order of one
halfwavelength.
I DOA: scattering near and around MS & BS will a¤ect the
measurements
I TOA and TDOA: results in a shift in the peak of the correlator
NLoS (signal takes a longer path)
I TOA based 400-700m error
MAI or co-channel interference

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Localisation with Beacons
Some nodes of the sensor network, are equipped with special
positioning devices that are aware of their locations (e.g., equipped
with a GPS receiver).
I These nodes are called beacons.
I Other nodes that do not initially know their locations are called
unknowns .
When these systems perform localization, the unknowns are located
using ranging or connectivity (also known as proximity). Generally, an
unknown can estimate its location if three or more beacons are
available in its 2-D coverage.
Once an unknown has estimated its position, it becomes a beacon
and other unknowns can use it in their position estimations.
The major challenge in localisation with beacons is to make the algs
as robust as possible using as few beacons as possible.
The resulting design consumes little energy and few radio resources
Limitations: they need a GPS or another positioning scheme to
bootstrap the beacon node positioning algorithms
Are unsuitable for use indoors
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 75 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Localisation with Beacons
Multi-hop
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 76 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Localisation with Beacons
In multihop positioning systems, nodes typically do not receive
beacon nodessignals directly.
Given the in uence of single-hop positioning systems such as cellular
networks and WLANs, it is not surprising that the rst multihop
localization algorithms tried to adapt single-hop technologies (i.e.
TDOA, RSS, and/or AOA information is collected, and the position
of each node is then computed using triangulation.)
Example: It rst determines the hop distance (called gradient) to the
beacons (called seeds) and, as a function of the average node density,
calculates the actual average hop distance to a beacon.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 77 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:

Localisation with Moving Beacons
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 78 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Localisation with Beacons
Using moving beacons in a system design can signicantly reduce
power consumption and cost.
In this type of system, nodes determine their own locations by
estimating their distance from moving beacons (also referred to as
mobile observers) in a coordinated fashion by applying a transform to
the range estimations to determine each node s position within a
global coordinate system.
Sensor nodes receiving beacon packets infer their distance from a
mobile beacon and use these measurements as constraints to
construct and maintain position estimates.
Also, once localized, network nodes can localize and track a mobile
object (robot) and guide its navigation. (Note: The mobility of
targets can be used to signicantly enhance position estimation
accuracy of nodes, even when the number of reference nodes is small.)
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 79 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Beacon-Free Localisation
Beacon-Free Localization
In non-urban outdoor environments, localization may be achieved
using several beacons equipped with GPS.
However, equipping sensors with GPS does not work in indoor or
urban environments.
In addition, the use of beacons, even assuming that sensors are
scattered randomly at the start, increases the cost of building a sensor
network.
In practice, a larger network may be designed to operate without
beacons, which is known as beacon-free design.
Such a design determines the position of every node via local
node-to-node communication.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 80 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Beacon-Free Localisation
Beacon-free positioning should be a fully decentralized solution: all
nodes start from a random initial coordinate assignment.
Then, they cooperate with each other using only local distance
estimations to gure out a coordinate assignment.
The resulting coordinate assignment has both translation and
orientation degrees of freedom and has to be correctly scaled.
A post-process is needed to convert the translation and orientation
coordinate assignment to absolute position information based on
reference information, such as information from GPS.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 81 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Some Comments on BEACON-BASED Algorithms
Some Comments on BEACON-BASED Algorithms
Assume that a certain minimum number of nodes know their own
positions through manual conguration or GPS. Individual nodes
location could then be determined by referring to a beacon s position.
All beacon-based positioning algorithms, however, have their
limitations because they need another positioning scheme to
bootstrap the beacon node positions, and they cannot be easily
employed in environments where other location systems are
unavailable; thus, they are unsuitable for use indoors.
In practice, a large number of beacon nodes are required to achieve
an acceptable level of position error.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 82 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Some Comments on BEACON-FREE Algorithms
Some Comments on BEACON-FREE Algorithms
use local distance information to attempt to determine each node s
relative coordinates without relying on beacons that are aware of their
positions.
Of course, any algorithm that does not use beacon nodes can be
easily converted to one that uses a small number of beacon nodes by
adding a nal step in the procedure, in which all node coordinates are
transformed using three (in 2-D positioning) or four (in 3-D
positioning) beacon nodes.
With three or more beacons, the absolute coordinates of all the nodes
can be determined at the same time.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 83 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Incremental or Concurrent Algorithms
Incremental or Concurrent Algorithms
1 Incremental algorithmsbegin with only three or four core nodes being
aware of their own coordinates.
I They then recursively add appropriate nodes to this set by calculating each
node s coordinates using measured relative distances from nodes with
previously known coordinates.
I Limitations: they propagate measurement error, resulting in poor overall
network localization. Some incremental algorithms can thus be applied in
later stages of global optimization to reduce propagation error. Escaping
from local minima and reaching global minima in the incremental stage
continues to be a major challenge.
2 In concurrent algorithms, all nodes calculate and then rene their
coordinates in parallel using local information.
I Some of these algorithms use iterative optimization schemes that reduce
the di¤erences between measured distances and calculated distances based
on current coordinate estimates.
I Concurrent optimization algorithms have a better chance of avoiding local
minima than incremental schemes.
I They can also avoid error propagation by continuously reducing global
errors.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 84 / 85

chunks.title: Localisation
chunks.content:
Appendix-A: Localisation in WSN Range-based or Range-free
RANGE-BASED or RANGE-FREE
Range-based :
I algorithms rely on the distances between nodes: this is known as
range-based localization.
(signal strength decay, TOA, or TDOA for internode range estimation).
Range-Free :
I While range-based algorithms require absolute point-to-point distance
estimation (range) or angle estimation for positioning, range free
algorithms do not require this information.
I In addition to measuring range information, range-free localization
algorithms achieve position estimation by solving a convex optimization
problem using a connectivity matrix of sensor nodes.
Prof. A. Manikas (Imperial College) Localisation v.21b 85 / 85
